relationship: The Hessian of the negative log-posterior in §3.1 is a Jacobian computation against the gradient; KFAC and diagonal-Fisher reductions in §3.4 are structured approximations to this Jacobian.

BNN posteriors are MVN near the MAP estimate by Bernstein–von Mises (§25.8 Thm 5); the Laplace approximation (§25.8 Rem 16) is the canonical construction lifted from formalstatistics into deep-learning scale in §3 of this topic.

Bernstein–von Mises is the Bayesian CLT — the posterior of √n(θ − θ_MLE) converges in total variation to N(0, I(θ_0)^{-1}). §2.3 cites this theorem as the asymptotic justification for the Laplace approximation in well-specified BNNs.

Bernstein–von Mises convergence is in total variation distance, hence in distribution. §2.3 invokes the total-variation framing to argue that in the large-data limit, the BNN posterior is approximated arbitrarily well by a single Gaussian centered at the MLE.

Weight priors w ~ N_p(0, σ²I) are multivariate Normal. The Laplace approximation in §3 produces a Gaussian posterior whose inverse covariance is the Hessian of the negative log-posterior at the MAP. §4 and §6 each carry MVN-sampling subroutines.

Hierarchical priors over groups of weights (e.g., one variance per layer) and the partial-pooling-via-shrinkage view inform §3.3's discussion of structured prior choices and §8.5's cold-posterior remark on prior misspecification.

The Taylor expansion of the AIPW score around the true nuisances underlies the §6.3 product-of-errors decomposition and the §8.2 Neyman-orthogonality derivation. The mean-value-theorem-based argument that the bias has a second-order remainder in the nuisance estimation error is what licenses ML nuisances in the DML framework.

The §9 instrumental-variables identification argument relies on the implicit-function theorem to ensure the first-stage map $e(Z) \to D$ is locally invertible when the relevance condition $\operatorname{Cov}(Z, D) \neq 0$ holds. The rank condition for IV identification is an inverse/implicit-function existence claim at the population level.

The §3.4 g-formula functional $\mathbb{E}_X[\mu_1(X) - \mu_0(X)] = \int (\mu_1(x) - \mu_0(x)) \, dF_X(x)$ is an explicit multivariate integral over the covariate distribution. The longitudinal extension in §14.2 iterates this integral across time-points, requiring the Fubini machinery developed in the formalcalculus multiple-integrals topic.

The §6 AIPW estimator's efficient-influence-function representation, the §7 TMLE rate theorem, and the §8 DML $\sqrt{n}$ result all depend on uniform central-limit theorems and Donsker-class machinery developed in the empirical-processes topic. The functional delta method underwrites the semiparametric asymptotic theory throughout §§4–8.

Logistic regression is the canonical propensity-score model throughout §§4–8, and GLM machinery (M-estimation, sandwich variance, score equations) underlies the §4.3 asymptotic-normality result for IPW. The outcome regression in §5 is a generic GLM-style fit, with the partial-linear Robinson model that threads the §13 worked example a GLM with semiparametric component.

The §5 outcome regression, the §9 two-stage least squares framework, and the partial-linear Robinson (1988) model that threads the worked example all build directly on linear-regression machinery. The §8 DML cross-fitting reference is Chernozhukov et al. (2018), which generalizes the linear-regression sandwich variance to ML-nuisance settings.

AIPW (§6), TMLE (§7), and DML (§8) are M-estimators / Z-estimators with augmented score functions, and their asymptotic-normality follows from the same Stefanski–Boos (2002) M-estimator framework developed in the point-estimation topic. The §4.3 sandwich variance and the §6.4 EIF-based variance are both M-estimator sandwich formulas.

Wald confidence intervals for the ATE — appearing throughout §§4, 6, 8, 13 — invoke the duality between hypothesis tests and CIs developed in the confidence-intervals-and-duality topic. The §12 Rosenbaum bounds and Anderson–Rubin confidence sets for IV (§9.4) are non-Wald CIs constructed via test inversion using the same duality.

The §9.4 weak-IV-robust tests (Anderson–Rubin, conditional likelihood ratio) and the §12 Rosenbaum sensitivity bounds are inverted from hypothesis tests of the null treatment effect under various nuisance assumptions. The §11.4 heterogeneity tests for CATE are hypothesis tests of constant treatment effect.

The mean-shift derivation in §3 uses the chain rule and gradient computation in $\mathbb{R}^d$; §4's convergence proof uses the multivariate Taylor expansion around a mode. formalcalculus's multivariable-calculus topic supplies the gradient / Hessian / chain-rule machinery readers might want to review if rusty.

Topic 30's KDE estimator $\hat f_h$ is the substrate on which mean-shift performs gradient ascent (§§2–3). §30.3's Nadaraya–Watson construction is the closest sister to mean-shift's sample-weighted-mean form, and the bandwidth-bias-variance trade-off in §30.5 is the prerequisite framing for §5's bandwidth-mode-count duality. The forward-link from Topic 30 §30.9 Rem 18 was logged in deferred-reciprocals.md:316 and is discharged by this topic.

The multivariate-Gaussian-kernel substrate (§6.1) and the Mahalanobis-distance reading of GMM responsibilities (§10.2) require the multivariate-normal machinery — covariance matrices, density form, marginal/conditional rules — that formalstatistics develops in its multivariate-distributions topic.

Topic 29 §29.7's distribution-free CI for the population quantile $\xi_p$ via paired order statistics relies on the same exchangeability + rank-symmetry argument that drives Theorem 1 here; conformal prediction extends rank symmetry from a fixed parameter to a random variable. §29.10 Rem 21 explicitly motivates Vovk-Gammerman-Shafer's distribution-free prediction as that generalization.

Topic 32's uniform-convergence theory (DKW inequality, VC dimension, Glivenko-Cantelli) is the asymptotic alternative to conformal's exact finite-sample coverage. The two routes meet at distribution-free prediction: empirical-processes pays a uniform-convergence rate; conformal pays only marginal validity. §1 here frames the trade-off.

The Marchenko–Pastur measure introduced in §5 is a probability measure on the nonnegative reals, and the §5.2 Stieltjes-transform derivation integrates 1/(λ−z) against the MP density. Comfort with Lebesgue integration on the line (and limiting arguments through the Stieltjes inversion formula) is the substrate for the §6 closed-form bias-variance integrals.

Provides the OLS substrate the entire topic builds on — normal equations, projection geometry, the bias-variance decomposition, and the condition-number diagnostics. The classical U-curve picture in §2.1 is read off this topic's parametric variance formula σ²(k+1)/n. §6's overparameterized revision uses the same decomposition with bias and variance components governed by Marchenko–Pastur integrals.

Discharges the forward-pointer this topic declares. §4's RidgelessVsRidge contrast is the explicit answer to 'what does ridge do in the overparameterized regime?' — the SVD shrinkage factor s/(s²+λ) smooths the threshold spike, and the λ → 0 limit is the ridgeless minimum-norm interpolator that the rest of §§4–9 analyzes. The reciprocal `formalmlConnections: double-descent` is already declared on the formalstatistics side.

The weak-convergence framework that all of §§2–3 lean on lives there: convergence-in-distribution as weak convergence of probability measures, the Portmanteau theorem (used explicitly in the Khintchine / type-convergence proof of §2.3), and Slutsky's theorem (used implicitly in the same proof and throughout §3). This is a NEW cross-site edge — the formalcalculus side will need a reciprocal `formalmlConnections` entry added when this topic ships.

The Donsker / functional-CLT machinery in Topic 32 is the framework for 'what happens to the entire empirical CDF as n grows.' EVT is the analog at the tail rather than the bulk. The technical machinery overlaps directly in the slow-variation arguments of §3 and the regular-variation expansions of §5. Topic 32's outgoing edge to extreme-value-theory is already declared as a deferred reciprocal in the audit dump; this entry resolves it.

The sample maximum $M_n = X_{(n)}$ is the extreme order statistic; Topic 29 treats the joint distribution of order statistics and the asymptotic theory of $X_{(\lceil pn \rceil)}$ for fixed $p \in (0, 1)$. EVT continues this story for $p \to 1$ (the maximum, §§1–4) and $p$ close to 1 (high quantiles via §5 POT). Topic 29's outgoing edge to extreme-value-theory is already declared as a deferred reciprocal in the audit dump.

The §2.4 Karhunen-Loève expansion expresses a centered GP as an L^2-convergent series of independent Gaussians; the §5.1 marginal-likelihood derivation invokes Gaussian–Gaussian convolution, a Lebesgue-integral identity at base.
GPs are infinite-dimensional multivariate Normals; §3.2's conditioning lemma and §5.1's marginal-likelihood derivation both reduce to multivariate-Normal density and conjugacy work that lives there.

The univariate-Gaussian density and Gaussian–Gaussian convolution identity are invoked throughout §3 (predictive marginals at single test inputs) and §5.1 (the marginal-likelihood derivation collapses two independent Gaussians into one).
The §1 parametric Bayesian regression starting point is the conjugate-Gaussian update from prior-selection; §5.2's Bayesian Occam's razor is one instance of the model-evidence framework.

GPs are stochastic processes defined by their finite-dimensional joints; §2.2's Kolmogorov-consistency proof bridges from those joints to a probability measure on path space via the extension theorem.

Rigorous foundation for the risk functional R(h) = ∫ ℓ(h(x), y) dP(x, y) as a Lebesgue integral against the population probability measure. The gap between empirical averages (Riemann-style sample sums) and population integrals (Lebesgue integrals) is the central object of every bound in this topic; the change-of-variables and dominated-convergence machinery developed in lebesgue-integral underwrites every convergence claim here.

Sister-site source of uniform-convergence machinery — Glivenko–Cantelli, Donsker classes, and the bracketing / metric-entropy framework that §8's real-valued function-class material specializes. Topic 32 §32.4's G-C-for-VC bound is the empirical-process core of the canonical Rademacher bound developed in §7.

The pointwise concentration in §2 is the WLLN with an explicit rate: for any fixed h, P(|R̂_n(h) − R(h)| > ε) ≤ δ. §3's finite-class bound and §4's Glivenko–Cantelli theorem extend the WLLN from a single hypothesis to uniform statements across an entire hypothesis class — the conceptual move that defines learning theory.

Uniform convergence (Definition 4) — a.s. convergence of sup_h |R̂_S(h) − R(h)| → 0 — is the right mode of convergence for the §§4–7 development. The §4 Glivenko–Cantelli theorem is uniform-a.s. convergence; the §6 symmetrization argument lifts pointwise mean-square convergence to uniform expectation control via Jensen's inequality.

Rademacher symmetrization (§6) routes the generalization-gap statistic through a sum of bounded mean-zero random variables — the CLT regime. The empirical Rademacher complexity itself converges to its population expectation at the CLT rate O(1/√n), giving the §7 canonical O(1/√n) generalization bound for bounded loss classes.

Hoeffding's inequality (§3) and McDiarmid's bounded-differences inequality (§7) are large-deviation statements: the empirical risk concentrates around the true risk at an exponential rate. The §3 union bound and the §7 two-step concentration both use these large-deviation rates directly, and the §11 algorithmic-stability bound applies McDiarmid to a different bounded-differences functional.

KKT subgradient calculus, soft-thresholding derivation from convex optimality conditions, Lagrangian duality between penalized and constrained forms, ISTA / FISTA convergence analysis via the descent lemma and Lyapunov-function arguments. The §3 FISTA convergence proof leans heavily on the descent lemma and the proximal-gradient inequality from this foundation topic.

The high-dimensional probability / concentration substrate — sub-Gaussian tail bounds, union-bound deviation inequalities, multivariate-integration moves used in the lasso oracle inequality's deviation step (§5.5) for controlling $\|\mathbf{X}^\top \boldsymbol\varepsilon / n\|_\infty$. Same use as in kernel-regression's §3.3 Taylor-with-remainder bias derivation.

Covers ridge / lasso / elastic net at the standard $n > p$ regime — KKT subgradient, soft-thresholding for orthogonal designs, coordinate-descent solvers, cross-validated $\lambda$ selection. This ML topic extends the parametric lasso to the $p \gg n$ regime where standard inference breaks down, adds the oracle inequality + restricted-eigenvalue / irrepresentable conditions, and adds debiased-lasso inference.

OLS, normal equations, geometric picture of projection onto column space — same prerequisite used by kernel-regression and local-regression. Reference for the §1 "OLS overfits when $p > n$" geometric hook and for the §10 OLS-baseline confidence-interval comparison.

The inferential apparatus — Type I / Type II errors, confidence intervals, p-values, Wald and likelihood-ratio tests under normal asymptotics. The §10 debiased lasso produces a $\sqrt n$-consistent normal estimator; the resulting CI / hypothesis test is the formalstatistics testing-theory framework applied to a high-dimensional setting, with the PoSI undercoverage demonstration and debiased-lasso fix appealing directly to the standard testing framework.

The bias-variance asymptotics in §3-§4 integrate the kernel against the design density f_X(x) dx on the support of X. The change-of-variables substitution u = (z-x)/h used throughout §3.2 and the d-dimensional volume scaling h^d in §6.3 are standard multivariate-integration moves; the §6.1 product kernel R(K) = R(K_1)^d uses Fubini.

The §3.2 second-order Taylor expansion of f_X(x+hu) and g(x+hu) = m(x+hu)f_X(x+hu) around x, with explicit h² remainder term, is the load-bearing technical move of the entire bias derivation. The §6.3 multivariate version uses the same expansion with the Hessian ∇²m replacing the second derivative. §8.2's local-linear bias also leans on Taylor.

The §3 bias and §4 variance derivations are direct lifts of the kernel-density-estimation machinery applied to the conditional-mean ratio m̂_h(x) = r̂(x)/f̂_X(x). Same kernel moment conditions (∫K = 1, ∫uK = 0, ∫u²K = μ_2), same h² bias / 1/(nh) variance rates, and the Silverman bandwidth heuristic in §5.1 is the kernel-density-estimation rule applied directly.

The §2.3 reframing of NW as degree-zero local polynomial regression draws directly on weighted-least-squares theory from linear regression. The §8.2 local-linear extension is locally-weighted OLS with two parameters (intercept + slope); its WLS solution generalizes the single-coefficient form via the kernel-weighted normal equations.

Multivariate-integration substrate for the §4 bias-variance computations and the §8 multivariate full-degree expansion. The change-of-variables u = (z-x)/h that converts the kernel-weighted moments into kernel-moment integrals, and the boundary-truncated form mu_j^(c) = integral over [-c, infty), are standard multivariate-integration moves used throughout the bias derivations.

Taylor expansion to order p+1 is the load-bearing technical move of the §4 bias derivation at general degree p — higher-order than the order-2 expansion that kernel-regression's NW analysis uses. The §4.1 residual-decomposition argument uses the column-space projection identity to absorb the first p+1 Taylor terms, leaving only the (p+1)-st derivative remainder to drive the bias.

Locally-weighted least squares with a degree-p polynomial design matrix is this topic's central machinery. The (X^T W X)^{-1} X^T W y normal equations are the linear-regression formulas applied at every evaluation point with location-adaptive design and weight matrices; the §3 equivalent-kernel formulation is the classical projection-onto-column-space picture from linear regression read in kernel-weighted units.

Kernel families and bandwidth-moment conditions mu_2(K), R(K) port directly from KDE — same K, same h, same kernel constants. The bandwidth-selection methods (Silverman, plug-in, LOO-CV, GCV) transfer to local polynomial regression via the same linear-smoother machinery; the §3.3 moment-matching identities are the local-polynomial analog of KDE's higher-order kernels.

The structural backbone of the entire topic. §1's three-pooling-regimes geometry, §3's BLUP shrinkage formula, and §5's frequentist–Bayesian bridge all assume the reader has internalized the partial-pooling theory developed there — the Stein-style shrinkage, the hierarchical model class, the James–Stein admissibility result that explains why shrinkage estimators dominate unbiased ones in sum-of-squares risk.

§2.4's GLS-as-BLUE / Aitken's theorem builds on the OLS / Gauss–Markov machinery developed there. The proof we sketch reduces GLS to OLS on a whitened system, which presupposes the standard OLS result that whitening preserves BLUE — proved in linear-regression as a consequence of the Gauss–Markov theorem.

§4.1's discussion of the ML downward bias for variance components is the application here of the general MLE small-sample bias theory developed there. The N-vs-(N-p) degrees-of-freedom correction is the prototype, and the §4.2 REML correction is the mixed-effects generalization.

The conditional-Gaussian identity used in §3 to derive the BLUP-as-posterior-mean reading — Cov(u, y) Cov(y)^-1 (y - E[y]) — is the standard multivariate-Normal conditioning formula, taught there in pure-probability form. The §2 marginal-distribution theorem also reduces to a standard MVN result via affine transformations.

§5.1's prior choices are weakly-informative priors per Gelman 2006, and the prior–likelihood–posterior decomposition of §5's PyMC fit relies on the Bayesian framework developed there. The flat-improper-prior marginalization in §4.2's REML derivation is also the simplest case of the improper-prior theory it covers.

Most of this topic is linear-Gaussian, so generalized-linear-models is best read as a related extension rather than a strict prerequisite. It becomes directly relevant in §6.2, where the discussion broadens from linear mixed models to GLMMs — the fixed-effects side inherits the link-function and exponential-family machinery developed there, and the marginal-likelihood factorization (4.2) generalizes but no longer factors closed-form.

The substitution rule for densities under a diffeomorphism — every flow's log-density formula in this topic is downstream of $p_X(x) = p_Z(T^{-1}(x)) \cdot |\det \partial T^{-1}/\partial x|$. We derive the $d$-dimensional version in §2.2 by taking the substitution rule as a load-bearing tool from formalcalculus.

The Jacobian determinant as the local volume-distortion factor, and the architectural design pressure for triangular Jacobians (coupling layers in §4, autoregressive flows in §5). Flows engineer their Jacobian structure to make $\log|\det \partial T/\partial z|$ computable in $O(d)$ time instead of $O(d^3)$.

Measure-theoretic foundation for densities, pushforwards, and the absolute-continuity hypothesis that makes the change-of-variables formula meaningful. We use the framework implicitly: the pushforward of an absolutely continuous measure under a diffeomorphism is again absolutely continuous, and its density is given by the Jacobian-determinant formula of §2.2.

The inverse-function theorem underpins the local-invertibility argument that justifies parameterizing flows by their forward direction: if $\det \partial T/\partial z > 0$ everywhere, $T$ is locally a diffeomorphism with smooth inverse, and the algebraic invertibility of the layer parameterization (§4.5) extends this to global invertibility.

Nonparametric density estimation by averaging kernel functions at training points; flows are the neural-parametric heir to this classical method. §10.2 contrasts the two: KDE's minimax rate degrades polynomially with dimension (the curse), while flows achieve parametric $O(n^{-1/2})$ at the cost of model-misspecification bias when the target lies outside the representable class.

The training objective in §7 is plain MLE applied to a flow's parametric family. Consistency, asymptotic efficiency, the bias-variance trade-off, and the consistency-under-regularity-conditions theorem all inherit directly from classical MLE theory; flows are just a particular choice of parametric family that supports closed-form gradient computation.

Standard multivariate Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(0, I_d)$ as the base distribution, the multivariate change-of-variables for the pushforward, and the joint / marginal / conditional decomposition used in the autoregressive form (§5). Multivariate Gaussian theory underlies almost every step of the flow framework.

Foundational framework for continuous random variables, distributions, and densities. Flows model continuous random variables $X$ on $\mathbb{R}^d$ as deterministic transformations $X = T_\phi(Z)$ of a base random variable $Z \sim p_Z$; the basic theory of $X$, $Z$, and their densities is taken from this prerequisite.

Catalog of continuous probability distributions (Gaussian, mixtures, Student-$t$, and so on) along with their densities and standard properties. Each trained flow $p_\phi$ defines a continuous distribution on $\mathbb{R}^d$ whose density we evaluate via §2's change-of-variables formula; the §1 trichotomy and §10's KDE/GMM contrasts position flows within this catalog.

The Radon–Nikodym derivative dQ/dP — the central object of the change-of-measure inequality (§3) — requires the Lebesgue integral to define and the change-of-variables formula to manipulate. PAC-Bayes uses dQ/dP as the bridge between P-expectations and Q-expectations throughout §§3–6. Every appearance of E_Q[·] = ∫ · dQ in the proofs is a Lebesgue integral against the posterior measure.

The Donsker–Varadhan variational form of KL divergence (§3.3 Theorem 1) depends on the existence of the Radon–Nikodym derivative dQ/dP whenever Q is absolutely continuous w.r.t. P. This is the technical condition that makes the master tool of §3 well-defined, and consequently every PAC-Bayes bound presumes Q ≪ P from §2.3 onward.

Reciprocates an existing forward-pointer from formalStatistics §32, which declares PAC-Bayes as the posterior-averaged refinement of its function-class complexity arguments. PAC-Bayes inherits the empirical-process viewpoint — uniform deviations of empirical means from population means over function classes — and replaces the supremum over H with an expectation under Q, charging the drift KL(Q ∥ P) instead of the function-class complexity.

Maurer's 2004 moment lemma (§4.2 Lemma 1) and the Cramér-transform machinery underlying §4's MGF-based bound chain are the canonical large-deviations tools. PAC-Bayes is a posterior-averaged refinement of Cramér's theorem — the Donsker–Varadhan variational form of §3 IS the Legendre transform of relative entropy on the function space of bounded measurable functions.

KL divergence is an expected log-density-ratio (§3.1), and the moment generating function of the empirical risk is the technical workhorse of every bound proof in §§4–6. Both depend on the expectation-and-moments machinery developed there — in particular Hoeffding's MGF bound for bounded random variables, used directly in §6's Catoni proof.

The √(1/n) rate that all PAC-Bayes bounds achieve in the agnostic regime is the standard rate of convergence in probability for sample means of bounded random variables — the canonical statement of modes-of-convergence at the rate the kl-form and McAllester forms recover. The §7 fast-rate refinements give O(1/n) under low-variance conditions, which is the L²-convergence regime.

The formal foundation for both HL-style test-inversion (§4) and the conformal-quantile threshold (§2.3). The duality between a level-$\alpha$ test and a $(1-\alpha)$ confidence region is the abstract machinery behind every interval construction in this topic.

Split-conformal's quantile of conformity scores (Definition 7), QR's empirical conditional-quantile estimator (§3), and HL's Walsh-average ordering (§4) all rest on order-statistic theory. The asymptotic theory of empirical quantiles underlies the stability argument in Theorem 5.1 and the HL/conformal equivalence in Theorem 5.3.

The asymptotic alternative to finite-sample exchangeability arguments. The bootstrap discussion in §7.1 leans on Edgeworth expansions, the QR asymptotics cited in §3.3 use empirical-process limit theorems, and Theorem 5.1's proof appeals to uniform stability of empirical-quantile order statistics.

Self-contained treatment of the bootstrap principle, percentile and BCa intervals, and the conditions under which bootstrap-percentile intervals are valid — the third resampling-based interval-construction method, contrasted with conformal/CQR/HL in §7.1 of this topic.

§3.1's change-of-variables theorem for densities (Theorem 2) is a direct application of the multivariable change-of-variables formula for integrals. The Jacobian determinant calculation and the substitution argument used in the proof are taught there in pure-calculus form.

§3 computes Jacobians for the log, logit, and stick-breaking transforms throughout. The Jacobian-as-linearization view and the determinant interpretation as local volume scaling — both essential for understanding why the Jacobian factor appears in (3.1) — are the substrate developed in that topic.

§1.1's conjugate Beta-Binomial computation and §5.1's hierarchical prior specification both lean on the prior–likelihood–posterior decomposition that bayesian-foundations-and-prior-selection develops in detail. PP automates the posterior computation that topic does by hand.

§4.2 sketches NUTS operationally and defers convergence theory and detailed-balance proofs to bayesian-computation-and-mcmc. The leapfrog integrator, dual-averaging step-size adaptation, and divergent-transition concept used in §5.2's funnel diagnosis are all developed there.

§5's eight-schools example IS a hierarchical model with partial pooling. The hierarchical-bayes-and-partial-pooling topic develops the partial-pooling theory — when the hierarchical structure improves estimates over no-pooling and complete-pooling alternatives, and how partial pooling shrinks group-level estimates toward the hyperprior mean.

The OLS analog. Theorem 3's asymptotic normality of $\sqrt{n}(\hat\beta(\tau) - \beta(\tau)) \Rightarrow N(0, \tau(1-\tau)\, D^{-1} \Omega D^{-1})$ is the QR replacement of OLS's $\sqrt{n}(\hat\beta_{OLS} - \beta) \Rightarrow N(0, \sigma^2 (X'X)^{-1})$. The Bernoulli factor $\tau(1-\tau)$ plays the role of $\sigma^2$; the density-weighted Gram matrix $D(\tau)$ sandwich-replaces $(X'X)^{-1}$. Reading linear-regression first makes the structural parallel transparent.

The no-covariate special case. With a single intercept feature, the KB78 estimator reduces to the empirical $\tau$-quantile of $\{Y_i\}$; Theorem 1 reduces to the classical pinball-loss minimization characterization (Topic 29 §29.4); Theorem 3 reduces to the standard Bahadur–Ghosh asymptotics (Topic 29 §29.6). Topic 29 §29.10 Remark 19 explicitly motivates QR as the covariate-conditional extension.

Theorem 3's full proof runs through Knight 1998's algebraic identity plus an empirical-process limit of the rescaled QR objective; the argmin lemma is the continuous-mapping step. Theorem 4's restricted-eigenvalue argument and oracle inequality both use empirical-process tools (covering numbers, self-normalized concentration). Topic 32 §32.5 covers the full scaffold.

Quantile regression minimises the check loss $\rho_\tau(u) = u(\tau - \mathbb{1}\{u < 0\})$; its $\psi$ is non-smooth but falls within the general M-estimation framework (modulo regularity). The scalar-intercept case is Topic 29 §29.4 / §29.6; quantile regression is the covariate-conditional extension. The mutual-relevance link is M-estimation framing — neither side is a strict prerequisite for the other.

Provides the H₀/H₁ framework, type-I error, $p$-value definition, and level-vs-size distinction that every section of this topic deploys without redefinition. The permutation-test exactness theorem in §2 is a finite-sample analog of the level-α framework developed there; the worked-example structure of §1 mirrors the same setup.

Ranks are a deterministic function of order statistics — $R_i$ is essentially the inverse of the order-statistic indexing. §3's Mann-Whitney variance derivation uses joint probabilities of order statistics; §6's Hodges-Lehmann CI inverts a rank statistic into a confidence interval expressible directly in terms of Walsh-average order statistics.

Required for the asymptotic null distributions throughout: the normal approximation to $W^+$ in §1, $U$ in §3, and the multivariate finite-population CLT (Hájek 1960) that powers §4's chi-squared limit. §5's Pitman ARE proof leans on the joint CLT under contiguous local alternatives plus Hoeffding's U-statistic CLT for the off-diagonal sum.

The Neyman-Pearson optimal test under correctly-specified parametric models — i.e., the $t$-test under normality — is the parametric foil against which §5's ARE compares Wilcoxon. The ARE framework asks: how much power do we lose by giving up the parametric optimum to gain distribution-freeness? The 0.955 figure says: very little under normality.

§6's Hodges-Lehmann distribution-free CI is the test-inversion construction applied to the Wilcoxon test, and Theorem 10's proof is a worked instance of the duality machinery developed there. The set-theoretic inversion 'a level-α test inverts to a $(1-\alpha)$ confidence region' is the load-bearing step in proving exact coverage.

Bootstrap and permutation are both resampling-based approaches to inference but differ in their null-hypothesis treatment. Bootstrap resamples with replacement under the empirical distribution (no symmetry assumption); permutation resamples without replacement under exchangeability (a stronger but more rewarding assumption). §7 contrasts the two explicitly when discussing rank-vs-raw statistics.

§2's $L^2(P)$-based geometry — tangent spaces as closed subspaces, orthogonal complements via the inner product, the projection theorem for the EIF — is a direct application of Hilbert-space theory. The §3 Riesz representation theorem and §4 Pythagorean lower bound both live entirely in the Hilbert-space picture.

The $L^2(P_X)$-norm convergence rates that govern the §7 rate condition — both for the nuisance error $\|\hat\eta - \eta\|_{L^2}$ and for the Cauchy–Schwarz bound on the product-of-errors $R_2$ remainder — require the $L^p$ machinery developed in lp-spaces §3–§7.

§3.1's von Mises expansion is the functional version of Taylor's theorem in a Hilbert space. §5.3's one-step asymptotic-normality theorem and §7.2's product-of-errors $R_2$ derivation both reduce to careful first- and second-order Taylor analyses of the functional $\psi$ at the truth.

The conditional-expectation machinery used throughout — $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X]$ as the $L^2(P)$ projection onto the $X$-measurable subspace, MAR's $Y \perp R \mid X$ conditional-independence statement, the EIF's pathwise-derivative computations against conditionally-centered scores — requires the σ-algebra foundations developed in this prereq.

Local Donsker theory and local bracketing entropy from §32 of empirical-processes are the substrate for the M-estimator asymptotics that the §5.3 one-step CLT builds on. §7's cross-fitting protocol provides the alternative when the Donsker condition fails for ML nuisance — but the connection to empirical-process theory is what tells us cross-fitting is the right escape route.

M-estimator / Z-estimator asymptotics — consistency, asymptotic normality, and sandwich-variance formula derived in point-estimation §6–§9 — are the parametric substrate this topic builds the semiparametric framework on top of. The §10 sandwich-SE derivation is a direct lift of the Z-estimator sandwich, adapted to Neyman-orthogonal moments.

The §4.3 recovery of the Cramér–Rao bound when $\Lambda_\eta = \{0\}$ requires the standard MLE Fisher-information machinery. The semiparametric efficiency bound generalizes the inverse Fisher information of MLE to the inverse efficient information of a semiparametric model, with the worst-submodel Fisher information as the bridge.

§10's Wald confidence intervals from the cross-fit sandwich SE follow the standard Wald construction developed in confidence-intervals-and-duality §4–§6. The duality with hypothesis testing carries over: rejecting $H_0: \psi = \psi_0$ at level $\alpha$ is equivalent to $\psi_0$ not lying in the $(1 - \alpha)$ Wald CI.

§7's high-dimensional sparse-regression case uses the debiased lasso (per high-dimensional-regression §12.1) as the nuisance learner. The underlying Bickel–Ritov–Tsybakov rate $\sqrt{s \log p / n}$ for the lasso comes from the penalized-estimation theory developed in §3–§5 of this sister-site topic.

§2's scale-mixture-of-Normals representation requires marginalizing the local scales lambda_j out of the joint prior — a multivariable Lebesgue integral over the (lambda_1, ..., lambda_p, tau) hyperparameter space. The Polson–Scott characterization (Theorem 1) of sparsity in terms of the implied marginal p(beta_j) presupposes the integral identity this formalcalculus topic develops.

§4's horseshoe-shape theorem (kappa_j ~ Beta(1/2, 1/2) under unit tau) is proved via change-of-variables from lambda_j^2 to kappa_j = 1/(1 + lambda_j^2); the Jacobian dkappa/dlambda^2 = 1/kappa^2 is computed using the change-of-variables formula this formalcalculus topic develops. §6's non-centered parameterization beta_j = z_j · lambda_j · tau is also a deterministic change-of-variables on the hierarchical model.

Sparse priors are the continuous-shrinkage alternatives to the hierarchical-Normal prior developed in §28.6 of formalstatistics. The horseshoe's local-global decomposition (§4) is the limiting case of a hierarchical-Normal as the hyperprior on the per-group variance becomes heavy-tailed; the regularized horseshoe (§6) reintroduces the bounded-variance behavior that hierarchical-Normal has by construction. The deferred-reciprocals ledger has hierarchical-bayes-and-partial-pooling's formalmlConnections to sparse-bayesian-priors pre-declared waiting on this shipment.

Sparse priors are downstream of the prior-elicitation framework developed in this formalstatistics topic. §6's m_0 hyperparameter (regularized horseshoe's prior expected number of effective coefficients) and §8's (a, b, xi) hyperprior on R2-D2 are concrete instances of the prior-calibration workflow this topic establishes; §12's hyperprior-sensitivity discussion returns directly to this topic's framework.

Sparse Bayesian regression specializes the linear-regression machinery of this formalstatistics topic to the p > n regime where ordinary OLS is rank-deficient. §10's diabetes benchmark builds on the linear-regression substrate this formalstatistics topic establishes — the design-matrix algebra, the noise-variance estimation, and the prior-on-coefficients framing are inherited directly.

§5's Bayesian LASSO comparison is the duality between L1 penalty and Laplace prior developed in this formalstatistics topic. The horseshoe is the prior-side analog of adaptive shrinkage; §10's predictive-log-loss comparison places sparse Bayesian priors directly alongside the ridge/lasso/elastic-net baseline this formalstatistics topic establishes.

Develops Bayesian Model Averaging as the textbook answer for combining models with a posterior over a candidate set; this topic generalizes BMA to handle the M-open regime via predictive-density-weighted stacking. The §27.10 Rem 27 forward-pointer to this topic was placed during that topic's drafting and awaits upgrade to a live cross-site link once this topic ships.

Develops AIC, BIC, WAIC, and LOO as model-selection criteria; PSIS-LOO is the modern computational realization of LOO that this topic uses, and the broader information-criteria context for the marginal-likelihood vs. predictive-density distinction is developed there. The named cross-validation subsection within that topic is the upstream for §3.3's exact-LOO fallback when PSIS fails.

Develops prior elicitation; the §4.1 four-candidate priors (weakly informative Gaussians, half-Normal scales, inverse-gamma length scales) build directly on that topic's elicitation framework, and §6.1's sensitivity-analysis use case is the topic-level application of those ideas.

Develops NUTS, ADVI, and the MCMC machinery; the §4 PyMC fits use the NUTS sampler exactly as introduced there, and §6.1's computational-averaging use case is a topic-level application of MCMC variability across chains and approximate-inference methods.
Halfspace mass $F(H)$ and functional-band mass $F(B)$ are concrete multivariable integrals; the §§2–5 derivations and Proposition 1's convexity proof both sit in this ambient setting.

The univariate quantile function is the foundational concept that statistical depth generalizes to multivariate distributions. The §1.3 axioms (D1)–(D4) are the natural multivariate axiomatization of $F^{-1}$, and the depth-induced ranks of §6.1 are the multivariate generalization of the order statistics developed there. Topic 29's outgoing edge to statistical-depth is declared as a deferred reciprocal in the audit dump; this entry resolves it.

The §4.1 consistency theorem (Donoho–Gasko 1992) and the §4.3 Massé asymptotic distribution are corollaries of empirical-process theory. The Vapnik–Chervonenkis Glivenko–Cantelli theorem on the halfspace VC class — the structural prerequisite — and Donsker's theorem for the same class give the limit law in three lines via the functional delta method. This is a NEW cross-site edge.

The §3.1 Mahalanobis-depth construction and the §5.1 DD-classifier optimality theorem (Theorem 5.2) both rest on the elliptical-symmetry framework articulated rigorously in the multivariate-distributions topic. The depth-of-a-density connection — that halfspace depth is monotone in density iff the density is elliptical-symmetric and unimodal — is the cleanest payoff of that perspective. NEW cross-site edge.

The Riemann-manifold metric tensor G(θ) in §10 is a smooth assignment of positive-definite matrices to points in parameter space; computing its inverse and divergence requires Jacobian-style multivariable-calculus machinery built up there.

The deep prerequisite — what makes an MCMC method correct, the diagnostic vocabulary (R̂, IAT, ESS), and the standard Metropolis–Hastings / Gibbs / HMC menu against which SG-MCMC defines itself. §1 of this topic frames SG-MCMC as the scalable wing of formalstatistics's MCMC catalog; §12 returns to that catalog for the head-to-head with NUTS.

The asymptotic-exactness statement (§6.3 Welling–Teh schedule) and the O(η + 1/B) bias bound (§8.3 Vollmer–Zygalakis–Teh) both rely on knowing how to interpret convergence in expectation versus convergence in distribution; the formalstatistics topic is the right place to sort that out.
The §12 head-to-head benchmark uses Bayesian logistic regression with asymptotically-Gaussian posterior by Bernstein–von Mises; CLT is the underlying mechanism for that asymptotic Gaussianity.

Polynomial-Taylor expansion intuition for the bias of $\mathcal{H}_k$ on the $\sin(\pi x)$ target: the $k = 3$ class captures the leading-order Taylor expansion $\sin(\pi x) \approx \pi x - (\pi x)^3 / 6$, which is why the bias drops sharply at $k = 3$ in the §1.5 bias-variance Monte Carlo. The general claim — that polynomial approximation rates control $L^*(\mathcal{H}_k)$ as a function of target smoothness — sits on Taylor remainder bounds developed there.

AIC, BIC, and MDL are parametric SRM analogs that share the training-error-plus-capacity-penalty shape but differ in penalty form (linear $d_k$ vs square-root $\sqrt{d_k}$), $\log n$ scaling, and asymptotic goal (prediction efficiency vs selection consistency vs description length). The §24.10 Rem 26 forward-pointer on the formalstatistics side declares SRM as the generalization to function classes; this topic discharges it.

Tikhonov, ridge, and lasso are concrete instances of the soft-SRM rule (Definition 4) with capacity functionals $\|\theta\|_2^2$ and $\|\theta\|_1$ respectively. The implicit nested family is the parameter-space norm ball, indexed by the penalty parameter $\lambda$. §7 of this topic reframes those penalty rules as continuous-capacity SRM.

Parametric substrate for the polynomial-regression worked example threaded through §§1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 10, 11. The bias-variance decomposition (§1.2) and Gauss–Markov variance bound (§1.3) are stated by name and used as given — the U-shape of MSE-versus-capacity is read off the parametric variance term $\sigma^2(k+1)/n$ from least squares.

Concentration inequalities (Hoeffding, McDiarmid) underwrite the per-class uniform convergence bounds that feed the SRM penalty. The §3.1 derivation cites Hoeffding via the FTSL; the §5 Rademacher SRM cites McDiarmid via the symmetrization-plus-McDiarmid route through generalization-bounds.

Theorem 3 of §31.3 (resampling consistency for the variance of a parameter estimator) is the load-bearing analytic result that turns the empirical variance of bagged predictions at any fixed test point into a consistent estimator of epistemic uncertainty. §6 of this topic leans on it as the formal justification for the bagging-as-UQ argument; without that result, the bootstrap-as-UQ story is only heuristic.

§2's ELBO-as-evidence-bound derivation applies Jensen's inequality to a Lebesgue integral identical in form to VI §2's; §10's bits-back coding expected code length is also a Lebesgue expectation. The integral identity is what makes the bound work for continuous-θ models — the discrete case is structurally identical with sums replacing integrals.

§3's Laplace approximation is a 2nd-order Taylor expansion of log p(x, θ) around the MAP, with the remainder term controlling the asymptotic accuracy. Tierney–Kadane's (1986) refined Laplace approximation that §3 references uses higher-order Taylor terms for marginal-density accuracy beyond the leading-order Schwarz form.

§3's Laplace approximation evaluates the negative Hessian of log p(x, θ) at the MAP as the posterior precision matrix; the determinant of that Hessian appears in the Laplace volume element and produces the BIC penalty when n grows. The relationship between Hessian curvature and Bayesian-evidence concentration is the geometric content of §3.

VBMS is the variational analog of the marginal-likelihood-based Bayes-factor framework developed in this BMA topic. §1's Bayesian Occam, §3's Laplace bridge, §5's Bayes-factor ranking on Bayesian regression, and §13's M-closed/M-complete/M-open framework all specialize machinery the BMA topic establishes.

VBMS is downstream of the prior–likelihood–posterior decomposition and depends critically on hyperprior choice. §4's Bayesian-GMM ARD result is sensitive to the Dirichlet concentration α; §11's computational notes return to prior-sensitivity diagnostics; §3's Laplace approximation is on the joint p(x, θ), which inherits the prior's curvature.

§9's annealed importance sampling is MCMC machinery — AIS uses Metropolis–Hastings transitions between intermediate distributions, and the schedule-length convergence argument depends on the MCMC ergodicity vocabulary developed in formalstatistics. The §9 head-to-head with ELBO is also implicitly the MCMC-vs-VI tradeoff specialized to log-evidence estimation.

§3's Laplace-to-BIC asymptotic bridge specializes the classical-IC framework this formalstatistics topic develops; §13's alternatives section places VBMS alongside AIC/BIC/WAIC/LOO, all of which trace through this topic. The BIC penalty −(d/2) log n that drops out of §3's Laplace expansion is exactly what Schwarz (1978) derives there.
The §2 ELBO derivation applies Jensen's inequality to a Lebesgue integral; the integral identity is what makes the bound work for continuous-$\theta$ models.
The change-of-variable formula in §5.3's normalizing flows is a Jacobian computation; coupling layers are engineered so the Jacobian determinant is triangular and cheap.

Bayesian inference begins with the prior–likelihood–posterior decomposition that VI takes as given; the conjugate-prior framework underpins §3's closed-form CAVI on Gaussian-mixture and exponential-family models.

MCMC is the asymptotically-exact alternative to VI's tractable approximation; §1.2's framing of sampling-versus-optimization and §6.3's no-free-lunch tradeoff both depend on the MCMC machinery formalstatistics develops.

Mean-field and structured VI scale hierarchical Bayesian models past the reach of full HMC; §4.2 stochastic VI was originally motivated by latent Dirichlet allocation, a hierarchical Bayesian model, and §3.4's GMM is its simplest case.

§2.4 Gaussian–Gaussian conjugate inference, §3.4 GMM component-mean posteriors, §4.5 mean-field Gaussian ADVI, and §5.2 full-rank Gaussian VI all use the multivariate-Normal density, its differential entropy, and the Gaussian–Gaussian KL identity developed in continuous-distributions; the §1.3 Beta–Bernoulli mention also lives there.

The default variational family is the multivariate Normal — diagonal in mean-field, full-rank in §5.2; the §4.4 reparameterization formula $\theta = \mu + L\varepsilon$ is the multivariate-Normal sampling formula.
VI minimizes reverse KL between density functions; the conditional-PDF framework is what makes §2's evidence decomposition a meaningful identity rather than a formal one.

§3.3's closed-form conjugate CAVI relies on the natural-parameter and sufficient-statistic structure of exponential families; expectation-inside-the-natural-parameter is the operational form of that structure.

§3.3 conjugate CAVI is the operational specialization of the natural-parameter / sufficient-statistic / log-partition framework the exponential-families topic establishes; Proposition 1's 'expectation inside the natural parameter' recipe is exponential-family conjugacy applied to the variational $q_j$, and §4.2's natural gradient is the Fisher-information-weighted gradient on the same exp-family manifold.

The §6 FTSL derivation chains a union bound over Π(2n) labelings on a doubled sample; the formal mechanics of event algebra, probability axioms, and the countable additivity that underwrites the union-bound machinery are the foundational prereq this topic relies on without re-deriving.

Hoeffding's inequality and the Chernoff bound from large-deviation theory supply the concentration substrate that, combined with VC dimension's polynomial growth function via the union bound, produces the agnostic PAC bound in §6. The Bernstein-vs-Hoeffding distinction underlying the realizable/agnostic rate gap in §7.3 also lives in this prereq.

PAC learning's (ε, δ)-formulation in §6.1 is structurally parallel to hypothesis testing's (α, β)-power calculation; familiarity with the testing framework's tradeoffs provides the natural intuition for the sample-complexity bounds in §7.1 (realizable) and §7.2 (agnostic).

Vapnik–Chervonenkis classes are the combinatorial analog of Glivenko–Cantelli classes; finite VC dimension is the discrete capacity precursor of the empirical-process theory developed on the formalstatistics side. The §9 bridge to Rademacher complexity is the natural entry point for readers proceeding from VC dimension to the continuous-time empirical-process machinery.
